topics.title: Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals
topics.description: Understand email protocols and attack vectors
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Understand email protocols
- Attack vectors
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Analyze email headers from a phishing email to identify spoofing indicators
topics.interviewQuestion: How does email work and what are the security implications?
topics.interviewAnswer: SMTP sends, POP3/IMAP receives. Key components: envelope (MAIL FROM, RCPT TO), headers (From, To, Subject, Received chain), and body. SPF, DKIM, DMARC verify sender legitimacy. Headers contain forensic evidence: Received chain shows email path, X-headers show classification.

quiz.question: What protocol sends email?
What protocol receives email?
What is the envelope?
What forensic data is in headers?
Why did the DNC phishing emails pass SPF/DKIM?
Which best describes Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals?
quiz.explanation: Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals is a foundational cybersecurity concept that defines how systems work. It is a security principle, not a programming language or protocol.

quiz.question: What is the primary purpose of Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals?
quiz.explanation: Security principles like those in Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals typically span confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

quiz.question: Where does Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals apply in real-world scenarios?
quiz.explanation: Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals concepts are universal across all security domains.

quiz.question: Which certification exam covers Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals?
quiz.explanation: Most major certifications touch on Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals at some level.

quiz.question: Best way to gain hands-on experience with Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals?
quiz.explanation: Practical application in a home lab is the most effective way to build skills. Reading alone is not enough.

quiz.question: Which skill is NOT directly related to Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals?
quiz.explanation: While web development may use security concepts, Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals is more directly tied to risk, response, and monitoring.

quiz.question: Scenario: You encounter Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals during a security audit. What is your first step?
quiz.explanation: A professional approach to Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals during an audit is to identify the issue and document findings for further analysis.

quiz.question: What is a common tool used with Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals?
quiz.explanation: Depending on context, Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals can involve firewalls, SIEMs, or packet analyzers.

quiz.question: Understanding Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals helps prevent which type of attack?
quiz.explanation: The specific attack Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals prevents depends on its context within the security domain.

quiz.question: How does Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals relate to the CIA triad?
quiz.explanation: Most security concepts including Day 1: Email Security Fundamentals can impact all three pillars of the CIA triad.

topics.title: Day 2: SPF (Sender Policy Framework)
topics.description: Configure SPF records for domain protection
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 2: SPF (Sender Policy Framework) is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Configure SPF records for domain protection
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Write complete SPF DNS TXT records for complex sending infrastructures
topics.interviewQuestion: How does SPF work and what are its limitations?
topics.interviewAnswer: SPF is a DNS TXT record listing authorized sending IPs. Syntax: v=spf1 ip4:192.0.2.0/24 include:_spf.google.com -all. Mechanisms: ip4, ip6, a, mx, include, all. Qualifiers: + (pass), - (fail), ~ (softfail). Limitation: checks envelope sender, not header From address.

quiz.question: What DNS record type does SPF use?
What does -all mean?
What does ~all mean?
What does include: do?
What is SPF's main limitation?
options: Checks envelope sender, not header From

topics.title: Day 3: DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)
topics.description: Configure DKIM signing for email integrity
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 3: DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Configure DKIM signing for email integrity
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Generate DKIM keys and configure signing for a custom domain
topics.interviewQuestion: How does DKIM provide email integrity?
topics.interviewAnswer: Uses public-key cryptography. Sending server signs email with private key. Receiving server looks up public key in DNS (selector._domainkey.example.com). Signature covers headers and body hash. If any signed content changes in transit, DKIM fails. Modes: relaxed (tolerates minor rewrites) and strict.

quiz.question: What does DKIM sign?
Where is the public key stored?
What key size is recommended?
What does relaxed mode do?
What did DKIM prevent in the BEC example?
Which best describes Day 3: DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)?
quiz.explanation: Day 3: DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) is a foundational cybersecurity concept that defines how systems work. It is a security principle, not a programming language or protocol.

topics.title: Day 4: DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Compliance)
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 4: DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Compliance) is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Implement DMARC policies
- Reporting
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Write a DMARC record and create a 90-day implementation plan
topics.interviewQuestion: How does DMARC combine SPF and DKIM?
topics.interviewAnswer: DMARC policy (p=): none (monitor), quarantine (mark as spam), reject (block). Requires alignment between From header domain and SPF/DKIM domain. Reports (rua=) provide aggregate XML data. Implementation: start with p=none for monitoring, then p=quarantine, then p=reject.

quiz.question: What does p=reject do?
What does p=none do?
What does rua specify?
What is DMARC alignment?
options: From header domain must match SPF/DKIM domain

quiz.question: What was the result of BOD 18-01?
Which best describes Day 4: DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Compliance)?

topics.title: Day 5: Phishing Analysis & Response Project
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 5: Phishing Analysis & Response Project is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Perform complete phishing incident analysis
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Analyze a phishing email and write a complete incident response ticket
topics.interviewQuestion: What is the phishing analysis workflow?
topics.interviewAnswer: Collect the email (.eml), extract and analyze headers (Received chain, SPF/DKIM/DMARC), extract URLs and attachments, check reputation (urlscan.io, VirusTotal, sandbox), determine user interaction, execute response (block sender, delete email, reset credentials), and update email security rules.

quiz.question: What is step 1 of phishing analysis?
What tools check URL reputation?
What is the response if user entered credentials?
How many user reports did the SOC process?
What should be updated after analysis?
